headline: India's Private Rocket Reaches Orbit, Opening Space to Everyone
meta_description: Skyroot Aerospace launches India's first commercial orbital rocket, making space more accessible and affordable for satellite operators worldwide.
summary_short: An Indian startup just made history by launching the nation's first privately-developed orbital rocket, promising to revolutionize how companies access space.
body:
Imagine booking a rocket launch like you'd book an Uber. That's the vision Skyroot Aerospace is turning into reality.

On Saturday, the Indian space-tech company successfully launched Vikram-1 from a facility in Sriharikota, southern India, reaching orbit in just 16 minutes. The achievement makes India only the third country—after the US and China—with a private company capable of orbital launches.

Skyroot co-founder Pawan Kumar Chandana describes the service as a "cab to space." Today, satellite operators often wait months or years for launch opportunities, squeezed onto large rockets with fixed schedules. Skyroot's small rocket, carrying up to 350kg of payload, changes that equation. Customers can book dedicated missions tailored to their satellite's needs and required orbit, rather than sharing expensive rides they don't fully use.

The test flight carried six payloads, including scientific instruments and satellites from an international client. It also carried something uniquely Indian: a diamond lotus and a tiny golden rocket holding microscopic sculptures of three pioneering scientists—a tribute to the visionaries who built India's space programme.

With plans to manufacture one rocket monthly from their Hyderabad factory, Skyroot aims to democratize space access. The company estimates 70-80% of its market lies globally, supporting services from agriculture to disaster management that millions depend on daily.
